political_offices.title: Delegate
political_offices.notes: Connecticut had never had a formal constitution — the old charter of King Charles II had served as substitute. At this Convention, Samuel Hart was present along with three of his brothers-in-law: William Cook of Danbury (m. Rebecca Hart), Orin Lee of Granby (m. Charlotte Hart), and Elisha Treat of Middletown (m. Lydia Hart). Hart and two of the three sons-in-law were tolerationists; Orin Lee was on the other side.

character.description: A man of great independence of character, proud spirited, and an earnest and effective speaker.
character.religion: In religion he was a very prominent Universalist.

cross_connections.display_label: Fellow Hooker descendant in same 1757 Fort William Henry alarm.

bio.bio_blurb: Revolutionary fifer, state senator, and Universalist

military_service.notes: Served as a fifer; DAR Ancestor #A051699.

narrative_blocks.header: Proud-spirited orator of ardent temperament
narrative_blocks.body: A man of stern independence and ardent temperament, Samuel Hart was an earnest and effective public speaker, sat in both houses of the legislature, and helped frame the state constitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He fifed through the Revolution as a youth
narrative_blocks.body: Born in 1761, he served as a fifer in the Revolution before becoming a prominent Universalist and state senator from the First District.

career.role: State Senator (First District); member of the legislature, both branches
career.notes: Delegate from Berlin to frame the state constitution; prominent Democrat.

narrative_blocks.header: Overwork unhinged him; he recovered to write
narrative_blocks.body: Cyrus Wadsworth Hart studied law so closely that he became for a time partially insane, then recovered and made his living writing and selling books in Ohio.

bio.bio_blurb: Wealthy Kensington farmer and state legislator

narrative_blocks.header: He farmed two hundred acres and made law
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Hart inherited his father's Kensington homestead, worked two hundred acres as a wealthy farmer, served as justice of the peace, and won a legislative seat in 1837.

narrative_blocks.body: Orren Hart Lee served as a drum major in the War of 1812 at the age of fifteen, stationed at New London, and later sat eight terms in the state legislature.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: The brick house he built c.1800 at 203 Hudson Street, Berlin CT — four corner chimneys, still standing.

narrative_blocks.header: Cabinet-maker who built his own brick house
narrative_blocks.body: Jesse Hart was a cabinet-maker in Berlin, Connecticut. Around 1800 he built a brick house at 203 Hudson Street, notable for chimneys at each of its four corners — each with a corresponding fireplace inside. In 1813 he purchased the tavern at Boston Corners and became its landlord.

cross_connections.display_label: Emma Hart Willard invited her to Troy Seminary and she stayed on to teach music and drawing.

narrative_blocks.header: She taught music and drawing at Troy Seminary
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Porter Hart was one of the Berlin girls invited to Troy Seminary by Emma Hart Willard, her relative, at Willard's own expense. She stayed on to teach music and drawing at the seminary. She later married and became Mrs. William Dodd of Cincinnati.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Troy Female Seminary on Emma Willard's invitation; stayed on to teach music and drawing.

narrative_blocks.header: James died young
narrative_blocks.body: James died in 1771 at the age of two.

narrative_blocks.header: A Massachusetts town was named for her brother
narrative_blocks.body: Her brother Rev. Theodore Hinsdale was the first Congregational minister of a new settlement in Berkshire County, Massachusetts — first settled in 1763 and incorporated in 1804 as the town of Hinsdale, named in his honor. He and Lydia were children of Capt. John Hinsdale of Kensington.

burial.plot_notes: South Burying Ground, Kensington, Hartford County, Connecticut. 41.60190, -72.80140. Body apparently returned to Kensington for burial.

quotes.text: Words cannot tell of the agonies I have endured. He found my fame and my own opinion of possessing talent above the common mind inconvenient to him, and he set himself systematically to work to bend and break my spirit.
quotes.attribution: Emma Willard, letter to a close friend, describing her marriage to Dr. Christopher C. Yates, 1839

quotes.text: We are distant relations, your mother having been of the same name and stock as my father... I knew and loved your father — he was our family physician.
quotes.attribution: Emma Willard, letter to James Gates Percival, March 19, 1832 — Yale Beinecke, YCAL MSS 703

institutions.institution_blurb: Founder and head of Troy Female Seminary 1821-1838.

landmarks.notes: Built 1809; her Middlebury home and first school site

education.degree: Studied then taught; ran the academy by 1806

military_service.role: Ship’s crew, then quartermaster, Connecticut regiment
military_service.notes: Captured by the British and later released

narrative_blocks.header: Captured at sea, he came home to heal
narrative_blocks.body: John Willard served on a ship’s crew at the outbreak of the Revolution, was captured by the British, and after his release became quartermaster of a Connecticut regiment. Only after the war did he train as a physician and open a practice in Middlebury, Vermont.

narrative_blocks.header: Jefferson made him a federal marshal
narrative_blocks.body: A committed Democratic-Republican, Willard backed Thomas Jefferson in 1800 and was rewarded with appointment as United States Marshal for Vermont, an office he held from 1801 to 1811. He succeeded Jabez G. Fitch and was followed by David Robinson.

narrative_blocks.header: A stolen bank key nearly ruined him
narrative_blocks.body: As a director of the Vermont State Bank, Willard was held liable when $28,000 vanished in 1812, drawing liens against his property. The money had been taken with a duplicate key; though the thief was eventually exposed and the liens lifted, his finances never recovered and he died insolvent.

narrative_blocks.header: He ran the business behind a famous school
narrative_blocks.body: When the bank scandal threatened the family, his third wife Emma opened a boarding school for young women, and John became its business manager. Together they moved the seminary from Middlebury to Waterford and finally to Troy, New York, in 1822.

narrative_blocks.header: Three wives across one eventful life
narrative_blocks.body: Willard first married Esther Wilcox, who died in 1788, then Mindwell Meigs, widow of Thaddeus Frisbie. In 1809 he married Emma Hart of Middlebury, the educator whose school would carry the Willard name into history.

narrative_blocks.header: His nephew judged New York from the bench
narrative_blocks.body: Willard’s nephew and namesake, John Willard (1792–1862), became a prominent New York jurist, serving as a justice of the state Supreme Court and briefly on the Court of Appeals. The two Willards, uncle and nephew, both rose from Guilford, Connecticut, to public prominence in the North.

education.degree: Medical training under a local physician (apprenticeship)

narrative_blocks.header: He grew up inside his mother’s seminary
narrative_blocks.body: John Hart Willard was eleven when his parents, Dr. John and Emma Willard, brought the female seminary to Troy in 1821 at the invitation of the city’s leading citizens. He had been born in Middlebury, Vermont, in 1810, the only child of his father’s marriage to Emma.

narrative_blocks.header: Schooled by the best, then West Point
narrative_blocks.body: His early education came from some of the country’s finest instructors, including the father of Bishop Huntington at Hadley, Mr. Adams of Bennington, and Capt. Partridge of Middletown. He completed his studies at the West Point military academy, which he entered in 1826.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried the school into a second generation
narrative_blocks.body: When Emma Willard remarried and stepped back in 1838, John Hart Willard and his wife took charge of the Troy Female Seminary. He remained one of the city’s best-known citizens until his death in 1883 at seventy-three.

parents.mother_research_notes: Huldah (Porter) (Riley) Lincoln — note double parenthetical suggests Huldah was previously married to a Riley
parents.father_research_notes: Simeon Lincoln of New Britain, Connecticut

notable.notable_blurb: Medal of Honor recipient, congressman, Baltimore judge

military_service.notes: Medal of Honor, Laurel Hill, May 8, 1864 (awarded March 30, 1898).

narrative_blocks.header: Rode to the head of a breaking charge
narrative_blocks.body: At Laurel Hill on May 8, 1864, with his column faltering under close artillery fire, Phelps rode to its head and led the assault to within feet of the Confederate works — where he fell severely wounded and was captured. His citation says he placed himself conspicuously in front of the troops.

narrative_blocks.header: The Medal of Honor came thirty-four years late
narrative_blocks.body: The charge was 1864; the Medal of Honor arrived March 30, 1898, when Phelps was a sixty-four-year-old Baltimore judge. He had passed through four political parties and three careers in the interval.

narrative_blocks.header: His mother wrote the nation’s science textbooks
narrative_blocks.body: Phelps was the son of Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps, whose botany and chemistry textbooks taught a generation of American students, and the nephew of Emma Hart Willard, the pioneer of women’s education. The family’s Hart line runs back to Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: His half-brother was outlawed by Davis
narrative_blocks.body: Charles’s father’s son by his first wife, Gen. John Wolcott Phelps, armed fugitive slaves at New Orleans in 1862 — for which Jefferson Davis declared him an outlaw — returned his commission to Lincoln on principle, and polled 1,045 votes for President in 1880. The half-brothers each wore Union stars.

Samuel Hart married I00029's wife's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099, b.~1697); she had married Nathaniel Hart in 1719.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Lieut. Samuel Hart married I00029's wife's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101, b.~1701); she had married Matthew Hart in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: Died when his only son was fourteen
narrative_blocks.body: Lieut. Samuel Hart had planned a collegiate education for his only son Samuel, but died when the boy was about fourteen. His widow, unwilling to part with him, kept him home and provided the best education locally available.

open_questions.question: Elihu Burritt descent through this line
open_questions.notes: CLOSED: Elihu Burritt's Hooker descent through this line is NOT CONFIRMED. His connection to the tree is through KINSHIP, not descent: his grandfather Elijah Hinsdale (X01931) and Lydia Hinsdale Hart (I00034) were siblings — both children of Capt. John Hinsdale (X00039). This makes Burritt the first cousin once removed of Emma Hart Willard (H00597) and Almira Hart Phelps (H00598). The claim that Burritt descended from Thomas Hooker through Huldah Hart (H00280) could not be traced through available sources. He is documented in the tree as an orbit_notable (X01932) with the Hinsdale kinship connection noted.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

parents.notes: Son of John Hart (X01887) who perished in the 1666 fire

cross_connections.display_label: married his son Nathaniel in 1719

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. FindAGrave Memorial ID: 77180356.

narrative_blocks.header: Away tending stock the night his family died
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1666 a fire set to the Hart house in Farmington killed everyone inside; John, then about eleven, survived only because he had been sent that night to Nod to tend the family's stock. He grew up the sole survivor of his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Three grandsons wed three of Hooker’s granddaughters
narrative_blocks.body: John's grandfather, Deacon Stephen Hart, had served Rev. Thomas Hooker's own church in Hartford. A generation later, three of John's grandsons — the brothers Matthew, Samuel, and Nathaniel Hart — married three granddaughters of Thomas Hooker, knotting the two families together within a few years.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandfather’s will singled the orphan out
narrative_blocks.body: When Deacon Stephen Hart wrote his will in 1682, he named the fire's lone survivor directly as his eldest son's son. The boy who had lost everyone kept his place in the family record and a small legacy.

cross_connections.display_label: Their families united — three sons married three Hooker daughters.

Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101
